config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically take priority over individual rights, because once the state or the majority gets too much power, it can easily excuse abuse in the name of “the common good.” From my perspective, a healthy society is built by protecting basic individual rights first, then balancing them with public needs when there is a real and limited necessity. In practice, I think the burden should be on society to justify any restriction on individual rights, not the other way around.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States we have seen how easy it is for majorities and institutions to trample minorities when they claim it is for the common good. Individual rights should be the default, and society should only override them in narrow cases where there is a clear, serious, and proven harm to others. That said, rights are not absolute in every situation, because things like public health, safety, and due process sometimes require limits. But the burden should always be on society to justify those limits, not on the individual to prove why their rights matter.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual, because in Indonesia I have seen how easily “public interest” can become a vague excuse for officials, majorities, or local power holders to silence criticism and ignore ordinary people. A society is not truly healthy if it treats individual rights as disposable whenever the group wants convenience, order, or conformity. At the same time, I do not see rights as unlimited, because public health, safety, and justice sometimes require real restrictions, but those limits must be narrow, transparent, and justified. The burden should always be on society to prove why a restriction is necessary, not on the individual to beg for permission to keep their rights.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over individual rights, because in the United States that idea has too often been used to justify discrimination, censorship, and abuse by majorities or authorities. A society is strongest when it treats basic rights as the starting point, not as something people have to earn whenever the group wants convenience or order. That said, I do think there are real cases where public health, safety, and due process justify limits, but those limits should be narrow and clearly proven necessary. The burden should stay on society to show why a restriction is justified, because otherwise “the common good” becomes an easy excuse to trample unpopular people.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual, because in Indonesia that logic is very often used to protect authority and punish dissent rather than genuinely serve the public. I have seen how “public interest” can become a convenient slogan for forcing conformity, silencing criticism, or ignoring people with less power, and I do not trust that kind of blanket claim. At the same time, I do not believe individual rights are absolute in every case, since public health, safety, and justice can require limits, but those limits must be specific, transparent, and strictly necessary. The burden should always be on society to prove why a restriction is justified, because without that standard, the phrase “for society” becomes a license for abuse.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States that principle has too often been used to excuse discrimination, censorship, and heavy-handed government power. To me, individual rights are the baseline, and any restriction has to be narrowly tailored, clearly justified, and tied to preventing real harm to others. I also do not buy the idea that “the common good” is enough on its own, because that phrase can hide abuse and let majorities steamroll unpopular people. Society matters, but it is strongest when it protects rights first and only limits them when there is a serious, proven necessity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
